metric_value: IBM is a leader in systems management today; eLiza positioning reflects starting from a position of strength rather than a standing start.
eLiza is NOT equivalent to or a synonym for e-servers; eLiza is a server management strategy, not a server strategy.
eLiza is not IBM server-specific; it will manage customer environments including solutions from IBM and others (IBM best/first supported, but not only).
eLiza self-management is transparent to developers — third-party software will not require modification using a whole new API set.
eLiza is products, not just a concept or an internal IBM engineering rallying point.
Computers are running the business in a fundamental way — without working computers, a business will largely cease to operate.
notes: Cited amazon.com and airline reservations as exemplars.

metric_value: The real-time enterprise has arrived — enabled by compute tools and decision support; just-in-time manufacturing requires end-to-end automated supply chains.
Manual backup procedures are increasingly unable to deal with computer failures, even in non-e-Business contexts such as grocery chains; the computer system IS the business system.
The computer system is the total instantiation of the business process; IT architecture implicitly defines time, place, and manner of employee/customer/supplier interaction.
In five to ten years, one of the big paybacks will be computers managing computers and running business processes automatically; people taken out of the loop, focused on exceptions.
notes: Prescient 2001 prediction; realized 2010s-2020s via autonomic/AIOps/self-healing cloud.

metric_value: Autonomic computing, AIOps, Kubernetes operators, cloud auto-scaling, and self-healing infrastructure realized this prediction in the 2010s-2020s, with further acceleration from ML-driven operations and AI agents in the 2020s.

Knowledge-worker exception handling follows: discover exception, query cause, make decision, transact to change, report/collaborate; eLiza self-management should perform all steps subject to preset policies.
Preset-policy pager modes: (1) problem fixed, (2) approve default procedure, (3) choose among actions, or (4) no notification needed — contrasted with low-bar 'wireless page that something is wrong'.

Message 1: Best Practices Automation — leveraging IT and domain expertise of IBM and others to address skills, expertise, flexibility, technology, and heterogeneity for more effective business processes.
notes: Proof points: improved quality of service, elimination of user downtime story.

metric_value: Message 2: Simplification — rooting out complexity wherever found by limiting and directing human interaction to high-level tasks requiring human decision-making.
notes: Proof points: reduced code lines, fewer unique IT specialties, eliminated admin.

metric_value: Message 3: Lower Cost — reducing overall costs, improving expense predictability, control, and accounting.
notes: Proof points: more servers per admin, fewer keystrokes per task, minimized over-provisioning.

metric_value: Stakeholder class 1: Line-of-Business (LOB) Executive — responsive to benefits of others' experience, improved total customer experience, domain expertise, predictable variable cost, improved ROI on IT.

Stakeholder class 2: IT Management & Staff (CIO, architects, developers, operations) — responsive to adaptable architectures, easier capacity planning, specialist-silo cut-through, lower lifecycle cost, reduced routine code.
Stakeholder class 3: Internal & External Users/Customers — responsive to services that 'just work', proactive handling, minimal training, self-help, passed-through cost savings.
Set clear parameters around eLiza: specific quantifiable 'stretch' goals (e.g., 90% reduction in management costs), milestones, and proof points; lay out in IBM statement-of-direction style.
Compelling need to brand eLiza — clear identity at non-technical level, logo (e.g., 'eLiza-enabled'); cited Budweiser iguanas as precedent.
Key part of process is defining what and when eLiza is; eLiza is ultimately things as well as a concept; does not exist today; first phase must come into being.
Sub-text to eLiza theme: 'this is not business as usual' — CEO/CIO-level pitch matched by dramatic proof points (e.g., reducing management keystrokes so one operator can manage 10x more servers).
Avoid positioning eLiza as 'great technology' (even though it is) — focus on business outcomes.
eLiza message to developers should emphasize masking of complexity — eLiza is not about mastering a thousand more APIs; it acts as a 'super operating system' providing a reliable foundation.
eLiza is about both e-Business and other businesses; assertion that e-Business is the harder general case may be true but will get lost in translation.

Service providers can generally be thought of as being today where enterprises are going — from an IT architecture perspective — but enterprises are more heterogeneous with greater legacy integration.
IBM needs to articulate proof points for specific areas of eLiza with strongest leadership positions; proof points are key to selling CxOs on ROI.
Make sure 'eLiza is heterogeneous' message is not confused with 'eLiza is based on standards' — heterogeneous has great pull with executives; standards are ho-hum.
Umbrella messaging opportunity: Pervasive Computing + Real-time Computing demands Managed Computing (i.e., eLiza).
For 30 years, MIS science has not been allowed to optimize the enterprise — 'we've always done it this way' remains pervasive; automating a mess yields an automated mess.
IBM can add key value at the intersection of eLiza and best practices from services — self-management of best practices.
Project eLiza Positioning and Messaging Strategy Workshop held at IBM Somers on May 16, 2001.
IBM engaged Aberdeen Group to conduct the Positioning and Messaging Strategy Workshop as one component of developing and clarifying eLiza positioning and messaging.
IBM E-server Analyst Symposium earlier in spring 2001 introduced initial eLiza messaging; this workshop extended messaging beyond what was delivered there.
